content:
First observe that if we round each \(a_i\) to its nearest integer we get all 7 numbers rounded up to 3, for a total of \(21\), which is \(2\) too large.  Hence we must round exactly two of the \(a_i\) down to 2 and the other five up to 3, so that the total is
\[
5\cdot3 \;+\;2\cdot2 \;=\;15+4 \;=\;19.
\]
If we choose to round down the two smallest \(a_i\), namely
\[
a_1=2.56,\quad a_2=2.61,
\]
then their “down‐errors” are
\[
|2-2.56|=0.56,\quad |2-2.61|=0.61,
\]
and the “up‐errors” for the remaining five are
\[
3-2.65=0.35,\;3-2.71=0.29,\;3-2.79=0.21,\;3-2.82=0.18,\;3-2.86=0.14.
\]
The largest of these seven errors is \(0.61\).  One checks easily that no other choice of two to round down can drive this maximum below \(0.61\).  Hence the minimal possible
\[
M \;=\;\max_i|A_i-a_i|
\]
is \(0.61\), and so
\[
100M \;=\;61.
\]
\(\boxed{61}\)

First observe that if we round each \(a_i\) to its nearest integer we get all 7 numbers rounded up to 3, for a total of \(21\), which is \(2\) too large.  Hence we must round exactly two of the \(a_i\) down to 2 and the other five up to 3, so that the total is
\[
5\cdot3 \;+\;2\cdot2 \;=\;15+4 \;=\;19.
\]
If we choose to round down the two smallest \(a_i\), namely
\[
a_1=2.56,\quad a_2=2.61,
\]
then their “down‐errors” are
\[
|2-2.56|=0.56,\quad |2-2.61|=0.61,
\]
and the “up‐errors” for the remaining five are
\[
3-2.65=0.35,\;3-2.71=0.29,\;3-2.79=0.21,\;3-2.82=0.18,\;3-2.86=0.14.
\]
The largest of these seven errors is \(0.61\).  One checks easily that no other choice of two to round down can drive this maximum below \(0.61\).  Hence the minimal possible
\[
M \;=\;\max_i|A_i-a_i|
\]
is \(0.61\), and so
\[
100M \;=\;61.
\]
\(\boxed{61}\)
